auditor: Claude Fable 5 orchestrator + 4 Opus discovery subagents (runtime, cli, meta, tests/ci) + 3 Opus fix subagents + Opus competitor scan
scope: Full second audit-and-improvement pass over the ai-agent-notifier package: hook runtime, CLI, config schema, tests/CI economics, packaging, error observability, and a bounded competitor scan.

checks_passed: npm test: 179 tests, 172 pass, 0 fail, 7 skipped (6 platform/daemon env gates + 1 Sentry live gate) on Windows/node 24
npm run test:e2e: 29/29 pass against real ntfy.sh with the real CLI (local Windows run, 29s)

Sentry verified via Sentry MCP, not code inspection: envelope smoke became issue AI-AGENT-NOTIFIER-1; a real hook run with a dead ntfy server became AI-AGENT-NOTIFIER-2 ('connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:9')
Isolated-HOME hook e2e: exit 0, stdout '{}', errors.log written; the fetch/undici libuv crash (0xC0000409) is gone after the node:https agent:false rewrite
GitHub Action SHA pins independently mapped to tags via git ls-remote: checkout@34e11487=v4.3.1, setup-node@49933ea5=v4.4.0
Pinned agent CLI versions exist on npm: @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.205, @google/gemini-cli@0.50.0, @openai/codex@0.144.0
All 10 workflow YAMLs parse (yaml.safe_load); concurrency on 10/10; paths-ignore on 4 live + 2 toast; push branches:[main] on 4 live lanes; agents-canary has no push/PR trigger
All four live drivers exit 1 with the correct key-missing message when keys are unset
npm pack --dry-run: 138.6 kB packed / 244 kB unpacked / 41 files (was ~6.95 MB with demo.mp4)
Dependency layering verified: production scripts import only from setup/, src/, scripts/lib; tests/e2e/helpers.mjs re-exports FROM scripts/lib
Honest negative result: graph lead 'README badge table references deleted ci.yml' was refuted — only scripts/live-codex.mjs comments did (fixed as TC-19)
Honest negative result: graph lead 'unit lane depends on ntfy.sh' was refuted — unit ntfy tests use a hermetic local HTTP server

CI (PR #4, checked with gh): all 14 workflow runs green on the final SHA — Unit 9-job matrix (node 18/20/22 x ubuntu/macos/windows), E2E on 3 OSes against real ntfy.sh, Agents smoke on 3 OSes, Toast Native (macOS+Windows), Toast Linux, and all four paid live lanes (Claude, Codex, Gemini real agent runs; Cursor wiring validation)

The first matrix run was deliberately allowed to be honest: windows node 18/20 failed, exposing TC-22 (npm test glob never expanded there); fixed via scripts/run-tests.mjs and re-verified green in the second CI wave
blocked: Playwright MCP UI verification: not applicable — this product has no browser UI (CLI + native toasts/push); nothing for a browser to drive
macOS and Linux toast delivery cannot be exercised from this Windows machine — covered by the toast-native/toast-linux CI lanes on push
Windows toast verification is subprocess-level (exit codes, ps1 parse, param contract); pixel-level rendering by BurntToast is out of process and not automatable here

findings.title: Hook entry point swallows every error with no log and status surfaces nothing
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:115-117 — `} catch { // Never crash — hooks must not block the AI tool }` is an empty catch around the entire main() body (parseInput/loadConfig/route/dynamic import/sends). No file is ever written on error; grep for appendFile/errors.log/error-log across src and cli returns nothing, and cli/status.mjs:28-92 prints only wired tools/events/config — it never reads or reports any hook error state.
findings.why_it_matters: This is the exact violation of agreed decision #1: the hook must not break the host, but errors must be written to a visible error log and surfaced by `status`. Today a failure (bad import, throw in route, etc.) is completely invisible — the user sees no notification and has no way to discover why.
findings.resolution: New src/error-log.mjs: bounded JSONL errors.log (128KB, keep-newest-half rotation) + logHookError in the notify.mjs catch; flushErrorReporting() bounded 1200ms before exit; `status` shows recent hook errors; opt-in Sentry mirror (SN-01).
findings.verification: tests/error-log.test.mjs; tests/notify-subprocess.test.mjs; isolated-HOME hook e2e wrote errors.log and exited 0.

findings.title: Promise.allSettled results are discarded — all per-channel failures are invisible
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:114 — `await Promise.allSettled(tasks);` return value is never inspected. Every channel resolves a boolean instead of throwing: windows.mjs:32 `resolve(!err)`, macos.mjs:9 `resolve(!err)`, linux.mjs:32 `resolve(!err)`, bell.mjs:15-16 `resolve(!err)`, ntfy.mjs:35/38/39 `resolve(false)`. So a `false` (BurntToast missing, notify-send absent, ntfy 403/timeout, bell.ps1 exiting 1) is silently dropped. This also resolves the graph's ambiguous bell.ps1->notify.mjs relation: Windows bell is invoked at bell.mjs:13-18 via `execFile('pwsh', ['-File', BELL_SCRIPT])` and bell.ps1:67 `exit 1` becomes `!err===false`, which allSettled discards.
findings.why_it_matters: The product's one job is to notify; when a channel silently fails the user believes it works. There is currently zero observability into which channel succeeded.
findings.resolution: trackChannel() wraps every channel (throw -> logHookError + {ok:false}); senders log their own failures at source: ntfy non-2xx/timeout/error, toast stderr per platform. Bell deliberately does not log (best-effort per design doc).
findings.verification: hook e2e with dead ntfy server produced errors.log entry `connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:9` and Sentry event AI-AGENT-NOTIFIER-2.

findings.title: Dedup lock is source-only, held 10s, and never released — legitimate notifications are silently dropped
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:16-34 keys the lock on source alone (`.lock-${source}`) and the winner never unlinks it; the only release is the 10s stale check at line 23. main() acquires it at line 78 BEFORE stdin is read/parsed, so it cannot key on event. tests/e2e/helpers.mjs:77 documents the consequence: `Remove a dedup lock so the same source can fire twice within 10s`, and tests/notify-unit.test.mjs:24-26 asserts a second acquire fails while held.
findings.why_it_matters: Two DISTINCT events for the same agent within 10s collide: a `task_complete` (Stop) followed seconds later by `needs_input` (Notification) both map to `.lock-claude`, so the second — often the more urgent one — is silently suppressed. The 10s window intended for millisecond-apart double-fires over-suppresses real activity.
findings.resolution: Dedup key is now source+event+sessionId[0..8) (sanitized); window 10s -> 1500ms; distinct events never suppress each other. Lock signature preserved.
findings.verification: tests/notify-dedup.test.mjs (key composition, window, cross-event independence); existing notify-unit lock tests unchanged and green.

findings.title: getToastBackend duplicated between hook path and CLI
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:36-41 and cli/test-cmd.mjs:7-12 contain the same platform->sendToast switch, differing only in import path prefix (`./platforms/...` vs `../src/platforms/...`). Confirmed the graph's 0.9 similarity lead.
findings.why_it_matters: Two copies drift: adding a platform, changing selection logic, or fixing a bug touches one and misses the other, so `test` and the real hook can dispatch differently — exactly the case where a broken toast passes `test` or vice versa.
findings.resolution: Single owner src/platforms/index.mjs exports resolveToastBackend(); hook path, cli/test.mjs and scripts/toast-demo.mjs all import it. Duplicates deleted.
findings.verification: tests/platforms-index.test.mjs asserts identity with each real backend module.

findings.title: Corrupt config.json silently falls back to defaults with no validation or signal
findings.evidence: src/config-loader.mjs:32-42 — `try { ...JSON.parse(raw); deepMerge } catch { // no user config — use defaults }`. The same catch covers both ENOENT (legitimate: no user config) and a JSON syntax error or wrong-typed keys (a real problem). There is no schema/type validation of merged keys anywhere.
findings.why_it_matters: config.json is the app's 'DB schema'. A user who edits it and introduces a typo/trailing comma gets defaults silently — their ntfy topic, sounds, and toggles vanish with zero feedback, and `status` (which calls the same loader) shows the default state as if intended.
findings.resolution: loadConfigResult() returns {config, problem:{type:read|parse|validate,message}}; validation quarantines wrong-typed keys (defaults win), emits renamed-key migration hints (sound->toastSound, ntfyPriority->priority) and unknown-key reports; hook logs config:<type> to errors.log; `status` surfaces it.
findings.verification: tests/config-loader-validation.test.mjs (7 cases); notify-subprocess corrupt-config test asserts exit 0 + {} + config:parse logged.

findings.title: toast.clickToFocus config key has no effect — dead/misleading schema key
findings.evidence: config/default-config.json:10 defines `"clickToFocus": true`, but nothing in the toast send path reads it: windows.mjs:20-30 always passes -ProjectName/-Cwd when present, and toast.ps1:42/94 always registers the agentfocus protocol and builds the launch URI whenever ProjectName or Cwd exists. The only reader is cli/status.mjs:33, which just prints a '(click-to-focus)' label. Setting `clickToFocus: false` does not disable click-to-focus.
findings.why_it_matters: A config key that looks like a functional toggle but silently does nothing is worse than no key — users toggle it, see the label change in `status`, and wrongly believe behavior changed.
findings.resolution: toast.clickToFocus now threads router -> windows backend -> toast.ps1 -ClickToFocus param gating both protocol registration and launchUri.
findings.verification: tests/windows-scripts.test.mjs pins the ps1 param and parses the script with pwsh.

findings.title: Empty catches in acquireNotifyLock can silently disable all notifications
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:19 `try { fs.mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true }); } catch {}` and 21-24 stale-cleanup `catch {}`. If the config dir is unwritable (permissions, read-only home, EROFS), mkdir fails silently, then openSync at line 27 also fails, the function returns false, and main() at line 78-81 treats that as 'another instance running' and exits without notifying — permanently, for every event.
findings.why_it_matters: A filesystem/permission problem masquerades as normal dedup, producing a total, silent outage with no diagnostic — the hardest class of bug to report.
findings.resolution: acquireNotifyLock suppresses only on EEXIST (true concurrent duplicate); any other fs failure fails OPEN with an explanatory comment — a rare double notification beats permanent silence.
findings.verification: New dedup test occupies the config-dir path with a file and asserts acquire returns true.

findings.title: Single `sound` field carries Windows-only identifiers used verbatim on macOS/Linux
findings.evidence: config/default-config.json:17,22 set `"sound": "IM"` and `"Reminder"`; router.mjs:19 passes `eventConfig.sound` unchanged into the notification. On Windows toast.ps1:109 maps it to `ms-winsoundevent:Notification.$Sound` (valid), but macos.mjs:7 injects it as an NSSound name (`sound name "IM"` is not a macOS system sound -> no/wrong sound) and linux.mjs ignores sound entirely.
findings.why_it_matters: One schema field holds platform-specific values that only one platform understands, so cross-platform users get silent or wrong sounds with no indication the value was invalid.
findings.resolution: Config key renamed sound -> toastSound (toast-scoped; Windows/macOS backends consume it, ntfy never does). Loader emits a migration hint for the old key.
findings.verification: config-loader-validation renamed-key test; router/platform tests updated; grep shows no remaining `sound:` consumers.

findings.title: Linux notify-send urgency is driven by the ntfy-named priority field
findings.evidence: src/platforms/linux.mjs:25 `'--urgency', URGENCY_MAP[notification.ntfyPriority] || 'low'` — the desktop toast's urgency is keyed off `ntfyPriority`, a field named for a different channel and populated from `events.*.ntfyPriority` (router.mjs:20).
findings.why_it_matters: Names are architecture: an `ntfy`-prefixed field silently controls a non-ntfy channel, so a user disabling ntfy or tuning ntfy priority unexpectedly changes Linux toast urgency, and a reader can't tell the coupling exists.
findings.resolution: ntfyPriority -> priority: the ntfy 5-level scale (min|low|default|high|urgent) is now the app-canonical priority; ntfy sends it verbatim and Linux maps it to notify-send urgency via URGENCY_MAP (min->low added).
findings.verification: router/ntfy/platform tests updated and green; loader migration hint covers the old key.

findings.title: Toast subprocess timeout (15s) exceeds the host hook timeout (10s)
findings.evidence: hooks/hooks.json:11,24 set `"timeout": 10` (Claude Code hook timeout is in seconds) for Notification and Stop, but src/platforms/windows.mjs:32 runs the toast with `execFile('pwsh', args, { timeout: 15000 })` (15s). bell.mjs:15 uses 10000ms, equal to the hook budget.
findings.why_it_matters: On a slow first BurntToast import the host can terminate the node hook at 10s before the 15s execFile settles; the toast is cut off and — because of RT-02 — the failure is invisible. The two timeouts must be reconciled or the outer one respected.
findings.resolution: Windows toast subprocess timeout 15s -> 7s (TOAST_TIMEOUT_MS with hook-budget comment); bell pwsh timeout 10s -> 5s. Whole hook stays inside the 10s host budget.
findings.verification: Constants asserted by reading src/platforms/windows.mjs + src/bell.mjs; full suite green.

findings.title: session_start silences toast and ntfy but still rings the terminal bell
findings.evidence: config/default-config.json:26-32 sets session_start `toastEnabled:false` and `ntfyEnabled:false` but omits `terminalBellEnabled`. notify.mjs:110 fires the bell unless `eventConfig.terminalBellEnabled === false`, so a new session beeps the terminal while the other two channels are intentionally muted.
findings.why_it_matters: Inconsistent default: the clear intent is 'session_start is quiet', but one channel leaks through, producing an unexpected beep on every session start.
findings.resolution: default-config.json session_start now sets terminalBellEnabled:false alongside toastEnabled/ntfyEnabled:false — session_start is silent by default on all three channels.
findings.verification: config/default-config.json inspected; integration tests green.

findings.title: Malformed hook stdin is silently coerced to an empty object
findings.evidence: src/notify.mjs:85 `try { raw = JSON.parse(stdinData); } catch { raw = {}; }`. A malformed payload becomes `{}`, parseInput then yields event 'unknown', route() returns null (router.mjs:9), and main() exits at line 91 with no notification and no record.
findings.why_it_matters: If a host tool changes its payload format or truncates stdin, notifications silently stop with no clue; this is the kind of upstream breakage the error log should capture.
findings.resolution: Malformed hook stdin logs logHookError("stdin", ..., {source, bytes}) and proceeds with {} — resilient but no longer silent.
findings.verification: notify-subprocess malformed-JSON test (exit 0, {}) + errors.log path exercised in e2e.

findings.title: parseInput emits sessionId/rawEvent/raw that the runtime never consumes
findings.evidence: src/parse-input.mjs:38-40 returns `sessionId`, `rawEvent`, `raw`, but router.mjs only reads event/source/projectName/cwd and notify.mjs only reads event.event; grep shows sessionId/rawEvent used solely in tests/parse-input.test.mjs.
findings.why_it_matters: Unused output fields imply behavior that doesn't exist. More usefully, `sessionId` is exactly the missing ingredient for a correct dedup key (RT-03) — it's produced but thrown away.
findings.resolution: Dead `raw` payload removed from parseInput(); `rawEvent` is now consumed: events that map to no notification are logged to errors.log ("hook event X from Y is not mapped"), making misconfigured wiring and new tool event types visible.
findings.verification: New notify-subprocess test asserts {"context":"router"} + PreToolUse in errors.log; parse-input tests updated.

findings.title: macOS osascript escaper does not neutralize newlines/control characters
findings.evidence: src/platforms/macos.mjs:15-17 `esc()` replaces only backslash and double-quote before interpolation into the `-e` AppleScript at line 7. Title/message originate from the hook payload (projectName from cwd basename, message template). A newline or AppleScript metacharacter would not be escaped.
findings.why_it_matters: esc() is the only barrier between attacker-influenced strings and an interpreted AppleScript. Real filesystem folder names can't contain newlines so exploitability is low today, but the escaper is incomplete and is the sole defense.
findings.resolution: macOS esc() strips control chars (\x00-\x1f, \x7f) before backslash/quote escaping, so newlines cannot break the osascript string.
findings.verification: tests/platforms.test.mjs escaping cases; suite green on macOS lane in CI (pending push).

findings.title: focus.vbs builds the pwsh command by string concatenation of the launch arg
findings.evidence: assets/windows/focus.vbs:13 builds `shell.Run "pwsh -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File ""<scriptPath>"" ""<arg>""", 0, False` by concatenating `arg` (the raw agentfocus:// URI passed by the OS on protocol activation). It is safe only because toast.ps1:100-102 encodes projectName/cwd with EscapeDataString, so quotes become %22.
findings.why_it_matters: The command line's safety depends entirely on an invariant enforced in a different file. A future toast.ps1 change (or any other registrant of the agentfocus handler) passing an unencoded arg would allow argument/command injection into shell.Run.
findings.resolution: focus.vbs strips embedded double quotes from the launch argument before concatenating the pwsh command, closing the argument-boundary breakout.
findings.verification: Code change reviewed; script has no automated harness (VBS) — covered by toast-native CI lane exercising the toast path.

findings.title: setup reports "Setup complete" and exits 0 even when tool patches fail
findings.evidence: cli/setup.mjs:229-236 patch loop catches per-tool errors and only logs `✗ ${tool.label}: ${err.message}` in red; then setup.mjs:252 unconditionally prints `✓ Setup complete` and run() returns normally (exit 0). No failure is tracked and the exit code is never set.
findings.why_it_matters: Directly violates agreed decision #1 (fail fast and loud, non-zero exit on failure). A user whose Claude/Codex/Cursor/Gemini patch silently failed is told setup succeeded, and any CI/script gating on exit code passes.
findings.resolution: setup collects per-tool patch failures, prints a loud failure summary, and exits 1; "Setup complete" prints only when zero tools failed.
findings.verification: e2e setup suite 6/6 incl. no-tools -> exit 1; grep confirms process.exitCode=1 paths in cli/setup.mjs.

findings.title: readJSON conflates missing vs corrupt config; patchers silently overwrite corrupt user settings
findings.evidence: setup/patch-config.mjs:8-10 `readJSON` returns null on ANY parse error. patchClaude:62 `const settings = readJSON(settingsPath) || {}` (same pattern at patchCodex:174, patchCursor:236, patchGemini:263). If an existing settings.json has a JSON syntax error, it is treated as `{}` and writeJSON overwrites it with only our hooks.
findings.why_it_matters: A single stray comma in the user's real ~/.claude/settings.json causes every non-hook setting to be silently discarded on the next setup. Fails loud-vs-silent and risks data loss (recoverable only via the timestamped backup).
findings.resolution: readJSONOrNull returns null only for missing/empty files and THROWS on non-empty invalid JSON, so patchers can no longer silently overwrite a corrupt user config.
findings.verification: tests/patch-config-advanced.test.mjs corrupt-JSON test flipped to assert the throw; suite green.

findings.title: status re-implements hook detection with a weaker predicate that never detects Cursor
findings.evidence: cli/status.mjs:19-23 detects managed hooks via `h._managed_by === 'ai-agent-notifier' || h.hooks?.some(hh => hh.command?.includes('notify.mjs'))`. Cursor hooks are written FLAT with no `_managed_by` and no nested `.hooks` (patch-config.mjs:246 `{ command: 'node "..." --source cursor --event stop' }`), so neither branch matches — a correctly-wired Cursor always shows "not wired". The predicate also hardcodes the tag string instead of importing MANAGED_TAG and is weaker than patch-config.mjs:44-47 `isOurHook` (which additionally requires the ai-agent-notifier/agent-notify path).
findings.why_it_matters: status is the primary surface users trust to confirm wiring; it reports a false negative for one of the four supported tools and duplicates ownership of the detection convention that patch-config.mjs owns.
findings.resolution: isManagedHookEntry/detectManagedEvents exported from setup/patch-config.mjs as the single detection predicate (incl. Cursor flat {command} form); status.mjs uses them and its weaker inline predicate is deleted.
findings.verification: tests/patch-config-detection.test.mjs; grep shows status.mjs importing detectManagedEvents.

findings.title: Update check runs on every invocation, is awaited before exit, and fires twice for status
findings.evidence: cli/index.mjs:56 `const updatePromise = checkForUpdate()` runs for EVERY command; index.mjs:80-82 `await printUpdateBanner(...)` blocks exit until the network call resolves (up to the 3000ms timeout at index.mjs:25). No caching anywhere. For `status`, index.mjs creates updatePromise but never consumes it (line 80 skips status), while status.mjs:85 calls `checkForUpdate()` again — two npm-registry requests per `status`.
findings.why_it_matters: Every CLI run (including `test`, `config`, `--version`) makes an unconditional network request to registry.npmjs.org and pays up to 3s of latency before exiting; it also leaks a usage/IP signal to npm on every invocation. Failure handling itself is fine (resolves null), but the frequency is not bounded.
findings.resolution: New cli/update-check.mjs: 24h TTL cache at ~/.ai-agent-notifier/.update-check.json, memoized to one fetch per process, offline-silent.
findings.verification: tests/update-check.test.mjs; UPDATE_TTL_MS constant inspected.

findings.title: Config writes are non-atomic — a crash mid-write corrupts the user's real settings
findings.evidence: setup/patch-config.mjs:12-15 writeJSON calls `fs.writeFileSync(filePath, ...)` directly; TOML is written the same way at patch-config.mjs:230 and :406. A process kill/power loss between truncation and full write leaves a truncated settings.json/config.toml.
findings.why_it_matters: The target is the user's live ~/.claude/settings.json and ~/.codex/config.toml. Non-atomic writes can corrupt the AI tool's own config, not just our hooks.
findings.resolution: writeFileAtomic (temp file + rename) used for every JSON write and both TOML writes in patch-config.
findings.verification: tmpPath/renameSync grep-verified in setup/patch-config.mjs; suite green.

findings.title: config and uninstall have no non-TTY handling — silent no-op hang on piped/CI stdin
findings.evidence: cli/config-cmd.mjs:59 and cli/uninstall.mjs:10 create `readline.createInterface({ input: process.stdin })` and call `rl.question(...)` with no TTY/EOF guard. On EOF (non-TTY/CI) readline closes without invoking the question callback, so the `ask()`/prompt promise never resolves and the process exits with nothing saved/removed. setup.mjs:110-141 already solves this with collectStdin()+makeLineShim, but the pattern is not shared.
findings.why_it_matters: `config`/`uninstall` invoked in CI or via a pipe silently do nothing and exit 0, with no error. Inconsistent behavior across commands.
findings.resolution: Hard !isTTY -> exit 1 guard on `config` (purely interactive). setup/uninstall deliberately keep piped-stdin support (e2e pipes answers and mandates exit 0); their real bug — hanging on EOF — is fixed.
findings.verification: Manual: `config` non-TTY exits 1 with "interactive — run it in a terminal"; e2e setup suite green with piped stdin.

findings.title: uninstall always claims success; unpatchAll swallows corrupt configs and reports nothing
findings.evidence: cli/uninstall.mjs:28-29 calls `unpatchAll(...)` then unconditionally `spin.stop('Hooks removed from all tools')`. unpatchAll (patch-config.mjs:364-410) returns nothing and at :375 `if (!data?.hooks) continue;` silently skips files that are missing OR corrupt (readJSON null). There is no per-tool result and no count of what was actually removed.
findings.why_it_matters: A corrupt config, a permission error, or simply nothing-to-remove all produce the same "Hooks removed from all tools" message. The user cannot tell whether hooks are actually gone.
findings.resolution: unpatchAll returns [{tool, ok, reason}]; uninstall prints per-tool results and exits 1 on any failure; corrupt configs are reported instead of swallowed.
findings.verification: uninstall anyFailed -> exitCode 1 grep-verified; e2e uninstall test green.

findings.title: getToastBackend duplicated verbatim between test-cmd.mjs and src/notify.mjs
findings.evidence: cli/test-cmd.mjs:7-12 and src/notify.mjs:36-41 are identical platform-dispatch functions differing only by import prefix (`../src/platforms/` vs `./platforms/`). Confirms the 0.9 graph lead.
findings.why_it_matters: Two copies of the platform-selection logic drift independently (e.g. adding a new platform or a WSL branch must be done twice).
findings.resolution: Same root cause as RT-04: cli/test.mjs now imports resolveToastBackend from src/platforms/index.mjs; the verbatim duplicate is deleted.
findings.verification: Import grep-verified; tests/platforms-index.test.mjs.

findings.title: ask/askYN/log helpers and ANSI color maps duplicated across setup.mjs and config-cmd.mjs
findings.evidence: cli/setup.mjs:16-40 defines ask/askYN/log with an inline `colors` map; cli/config-cmd.mjs:5-28 defines near-identical ask/askYN/log with its own inline `colors` map. Neither uses the existing `c` color helpers exported from cli/ui.mjs.
findings.why_it_matters: Three color systems (setup, config-cmd, ui.mjs) and two prompt implementations must be kept in sync; ui.mjs `c` is the intended single source for color.
findings.resolution: ask/askYN/log and the color map live only in cli/ui.mjs; setup.mjs and config.mjs import them.
findings.verification: Grep: all five cli modules import shared helpers from ./ui.mjs.

findings.title: test command exits 0 even when toast/ntfy delivery fails
findings.evidence: cli/test-cmd.mjs:32-53 calls `spin.fail('Toast failed')` / `spin.fail('ntfy failed')` on failure but never sets `process.exitCode`; run() returns normally so the CLI exits 0.
findings.why_it_matters: `test` is the natural command to gate a script or CI smoke check on, but a failed notification is indistinguishable from success by exit code.
findings.resolution: test command tracks toast/ntfy delivery failures and exits 1; uses loadConfigResult to fail loud on config problems; new `bell` channel test is best-effort (warns, never fails the command).
findings.verification: cli/test.mjs exitCode=1 on failure grep-verified; manual bell smoke: warn + exit 0.

findings.title: Circular import: status.mjs imports checkForUpdate from the CLI entrypoint index.mjs
findings.evidence: cli/status.mjs:7 `import { checkForUpdate } from './index.mjs'` while index.mjs imports status via `COMMANDS.status: () => import('./status.mjs')` (index.mjs:17). index.mjs also runs `main()` at top level (index.mjs:107). It works only because ESM caches the entry module so main() is not re-run, but the entry now doubles as a shared library.
findings.why_it_matters: Fragile coupling — any future top-level side effect in index.mjs would execute during a status import, and the entrypoint owning a reusable network helper is a smell that also drives the double-fetch in CL-04.
findings.resolution: status.mjs imports checkForUpdate from cli/update-check.mjs instead of the CLI entrypoint — the index.mjs import cycle is gone and the check no longer fires twice.
findings.verification: Import graph grep-verified (index -> update-check, status -> update-check).

findings.title: Dead exports and unused imports in the UI layer
findings.evidence: cli/ui.mjs:127 `progressBar` is defined but imported nowhere. cli/status.mjs:8 imports `table` but never uses it (table defined ui.mjs:79 is otherwise unused). cli/test-cmd.mjs:5 imports `banner` but never uses it. index.mjs:53/65/85 threads `gradient` into printHelp() which never calls it (gradient is only actually used internally by banner() at ui.mjs:148).
findings.why_it_matters: Dead surface area misleads readers about what the UI module provides and what each command renders.
findings.resolution: Dead table/progressBar helpers deleted; gradient/stripAnsi de-exported.
findings.verification: cli/ui.mjs reviewed; suite green.

findings.title: Spinner frames are all empty strings — the spinner renders no glyph
findings.evidence: cli/ui.mjs:98 `const SPINNER_FRAMES = ['', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', ''];` — every frame is an empty string, so `c.brand(SPINNER_FRAMES[i % ...])` at ui.mjs:107 prints nothing; the animation is invisible while the message still updates.
findings.why_it_matters: The spinner (used by test/uninstall) appears broken/blank; either the glyphs were lost in an encoding pass or the frames array is vestigial.
findings.resolution: SPINNER_FRAMES now the real Braille glyph cycle instead of empty strings.
findings.verification: cli/ui.mjs:130 inspected.

findings.title: Inconsistent command-file naming (-cmd suffix on only two of five)
findings.evidence: cli/ has test-cmd.mjs and config-cmd.mjs (with `-cmd`) but setup.mjs, status.mjs, uninstall.mjs (without). All five export the same `run(arg)` command entrypoint.
findings.why_it_matters: The suffix looks meaningful but is arbitrary; `config-cmd.mjs` vs `config-loader.mjs` is the only place the suffix earns its keep. Inconsistency makes the dispatch table harder to scan.
findings.resolution: git mv cli/config-cmd.mjs -> cli/config.mjs and cli/test-cmd.mjs -> cli/test.mjs; command files now uniformly match their command names; imports and help text updated.
findings.verification: git status shows RM entries preserving history; `node cli/index.mjs --help` exits 0.

findings.title: unpatchAll cleans a codex event (PermissionRequest) that patch never writes
findings.evidence: patch-config.mjs:367 lists codex events `['Stop', 'SessionStart', 'PermissionRequest']` for removal, but patchCodex only ever writes Stop and SessionStart (patch-config.mjs:190-191). PermissionRequest cleanup can never match anything we created.
findings.why_it_matters: Drift between the patch and unpatch event lists is a latent inconsistency — if the patch list changes, the unpatch list must be kept in sync by hand, and the stale entry suggests an event that was never actually shipped.
findings.resolution: Single TOOL_EVENTS map drives both patch and unpatch, so they cannot drift; the never-written Codex PermissionRequest cleanup path is removed.
findings.verification: Byte-identical patch/unpatch round-trip test added; TOOL_EVENTS used at all patch sites and in unpatchAll (grep).

findings.title: setup prints "Backed up originals" unconditionally and exits 0 when no tools found
findings.evidence: cli/setup.mjs:237 always logs `Backed up originals to ${backupDir}` even when backup() (patch-config.mjs:17-24) made no copies (all-new configs, or every patch threw before writing). Separately, setup.mjs:172-176 handles "No supported AI tools found" by logging red then `return` — exit 0.
findings.why_it_matters: The backup line implies a recoverable snapshot exists when it may not; and a setup that patched nothing (no tools) reports success via exit 0, again conflicting with fail-fast.
findings.resolution: "Backed up originals" no longer prints unconditionally; zero-tools-found is a loud failure with exit 1. The one e2e assertion pinning the old exit-0 behavior was updated deliberately as part of the fix.
findings.verification: e2e setup no-tools test now asserts exit 1 and passes.

findings.title: No SIGINT handling in interactive wizards; Ctrl+C can leave partially-applied state
findings.evidence: No `process.on('SIGINT')` or readline 'SIGINT' handler in setup.mjs, config-cmd.mjs, or uninstall.mjs. In setup, config is saved at setup.mjs:214 (step 5) before tool patching at :229 (step 6), so an interrupt between steps leaves saved config but unpatched tools with no message.
findings.why_it_matters: Ctrl+C mid-wizard yields an inconsistent, unreported state and no clean cancellation message; the terminal cursor/echo may also be left mid-prompt.
findings.resolution: SIGINT in interactive wizards aborts with "aborted — nothing saved" and exit 130 BEFORE any write; prompts are EOF-safe (no hang).
findings.verification: exit(130) paths grep-verified in setup/config/uninstall; manual Ctrl+C smoke by fix agent.

findings.title: Codex config.toml is edited by string/regex rather than a TOML round-trip
findings.evidence: patch-config.mjs:214-223 migrates/inserts the features flag with `toml.includes('codex_hooks')`, `toml.replace(/codex_hooks\s*=\s*true/, ...)`, `toml.includes('[features]')` and `toml.replace('[features]', ...)`; rebuildHooksState/stripHooksStateKeys (patch-config.mjs:126-169, 320-362) parse [hooks.state] blocks line-by-line.
findings.why_it_matters: These operate on raw text: a `[features]` token inside a comment/string, `codex_hooks = false`, or a foreign `[hooks.state.'k']` block with multi-line/array values could be mis-detected or mangled. It is a deliberate zero-dependency tradeoff, but the fidelity limits are real.
findings.resolution: Codex config.toml is still edited by string surgery; a TOML round-trip needs a parser and zero-dependency rules it out for now. TODO(audit CL-18) comment left at the patchCodex site per the deferred-issues policy.
findings.verification: TODO comment grep-verified at setup/patch-config.mjs:225; string-surgery behavior pinned by patch-config tests.

findings.title: No paths-ignore: docs-only commits trigger all 4 paid live agent jobs
findings.evidence: All 9 workflows use `on: push: / pull_request:` with no `paths:`/`paths-ignore:` filter (verified by repo-wide grep — only matches are in test comments). live-claude.yml/live-gemini.yml/live-codex.yml/live-cursor.yml each spend paid ANTHROPIC/GEMINI/OPENAI(x2) keys unconditionally. Recent history is docs-heavy (570e637 version bump, 6403eaa/2413389/c4d5d8f docs) — each such push ran every paid job.
findings.why_it_matters: Every README/version/doc commit burns four paid third-party API budgets for zero verification value; on an actively-edited repo this is the dominant recurring cost.
findings.resolution: paths-ignore [**/*.md, docs/**, LICENSE, .gitignore] on the 4 live lanes + 2 toast lanes; assets/** deliberately NOT ignored (toast lanes exercise assets).
findings.verification: Grep across .github/workflows shows the ignore lists on all six.

findings.title: No concurrency/cancel-in-progress: rapid pushes stack paid runs
findings.evidence: No `concurrency:` key in any of the 9 workflows (grep confirmed). Two pushes 30s apart run two full sets of paid live jobs to completion.
findings.why_it_matters: Superseded commits keep consuming paid API/agent minutes instead of being cancelled — compounds TC-01 during iterative work.
findings.resolution: concurrency group + cancel-in-progress on all 10 workflows so rapid pushes cannot stack paid runs.
findings.verification: Grep: 10/10 workflows carry a concurrency block.

findings.title: Paid live jobs run on pushes to every branch, not just main
findings.evidence: live-*.yml triggers are bare `on: push:` (no `branches:` filter). A push to any feature/topic branch fires all four paid jobs, in addition to the PR run.
findings.why_it_matters: Doubles paid spend during branch development (branch push + PR event both trigger), with no added signal over the PR run.
findings.resolution: Live lanes trigger on push only for branches: [main] (plus PRs), so feature-branch pushes cannot burn API credits.
findings.verification: Grep shows branches: [main] on live-claude/codex/cursor/gemini.

findings.title: Agent CLIs installed at floating 'latest' (unpinned) in 4 workflows
findings.evidence: agents.yml: `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code @google/gemini-cli @openai/codex`; live-claude.yml `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code`; live-gemini.yml `@google/gemini-cli`; live-codex.yml `@openai/codex` — all unversioned.
findings.why_it_matters: Green builds are non-reproducible and an unrelated upstream CLI release can redden CI, conflating 'our regression' with 'vendor shipped a break'. Partly deliberate (smoke-load `--expect launch-only` pins Codex classification to catch drift), but the floating install undermines reproducibility of the whole matrix.
findings.resolution: Agent CLIs pinned: @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.205, @google/gemini-cli@0.50.0, @openai/codex@0.144.0. New agents-canary.yml runs the same smoke-load weekly at @latest (cron Mon 06:00 UTC + workflow_dispatch, gates nothing) as a drift canary.
findings.verification: npm view confirms all three pinned versions exist; canary triggers grep-verified (no push/PR).

findings.title: cursor-agent installed via unpinned curl|bash from cursor.com
findings.evidence: agents.yml: `curl https://cursor.com/install -fsS | bash` (with `continue-on-error: true`, non-Windows only).
findings.why_it_matters: Remote script piped to a shell with no version/checksum pin — a compromised or changed installer executes on the runner. `continue-on-error` limits blast radius to a soft-skip but not the code execution itself.
findings.resolution: live-cursor.yml no longer installs cursor-agent at all — the lane validates config patching using OPENAI_API_KEY; the unpinned curl|bash install is gone.
findings.verification: Grep of live-cursor.yml: no curl, no install step beyond npm install.

findings.title: GitHub Actions pinned by mutable tag, not commit SHA
findings.evidence: Every workflow uses `actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-node@v4` (tag refs).
findings.why_it_matters: Tag refs are mutable; SHA-pinning is the supply-chain best practice for public CI. Low risk for first-party actions but flagged for completeness.
findings.resolution: Actions pinned by commit SHA: actions/checkout@34e11487… (# v4 = v4.3.1), actions/setup-node@49933ea5… (# v4 = v4.4.0).
findings.verification: git ls-remote independently maps both SHAs to their v4 tags.

findings.title: Package claims Node >=18 but CI only tests Node 22
findings.evidence: package.json `engines.node: '>=18.0.0'` and README badge `node >= 18`; every workflow sets `setup-node with node-version: 22`. Node 18/20 (the declared floor) are never exercised.
findings.why_it_matters: A syntax/API usage that breaks on Node 18/20 ships green — the compatibility claim is untested. (Zero-dep pure-ESM code makes this low-probability but the claim is unverified.)
findings.resolution: unit.yml matrix node [18, 20, 22] x [ubuntu, macos, windows].
findings.verification: Workflow grep; suite runs locally on node 24 too.

findings.title: bell.e2e header claims stderr verification but tests only assert exit 0
findings.evidence: tests/e2e/bell.e2e.test.mjs:2 header: 'verifies bell channel behavior via stderr output.' All 4 tests assert only `assert.equal(res.status, 0, ...)` — none inspect stdout/stderr or any BEL marker. It proves notify.mjs doesn't crash with bell in the dispatch, not that the bell fired or was suppressed by config.
findings.why_it_matters: A future agent reading the green 'bell e2e' suite would believe bell delivery/suppression is behaviorally covered end-to-end; it is not. This is exactly the 'asserts nothing meaningful' pattern.
findings.resolution: bell.e2e header rewritten to describe what is actually asserted; stdout {} assertions added.
findings.verification: File reviewed; 12/12 e2e helper+bell tests pass offline.

findings.title: live-cursor prints 'CURSOR_API_KEY is set' when it is actually OPENAI_API_KEY
findings.evidence: scripts/live-cursor.mjs accepts `CURSOR_API_KEY || OPENAI_API_KEY || ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, then logs `console.log('PASS (hard): CURSOR_API_KEY is set');`. live-cursor.yml only injects `OPENAI_API_KEY` — so in CI that line is literally false. Header comment '1. CURSOR_API_KEY must be present.' also contradicts the 3-key fallback.
findings.why_it_matters: A hard-PASS log asserting a specific fact that is false erodes trust in the 'strict, real' logs and could mask a genuinely missing CURSOR_API_KEY.
findings.resolution: live-cursor key-missing message names OPENAI_API_KEY (the variable actually read).
findings.verification: Driver run with keys unset exits 1 with the correct message (fix-ci ran all four).

findings.title: README headline + live-codex/cursor job names say 'E2E'/'driven end to end' though the agent never runs
findings.evidence: README.md:236 blanket claim '...the real agent CLIs installed from npm and driven end to end...'. live-codex.yml `name: Live Codex E2E (required)` and live-cursor.yml `name: Live Cursor E2E (required)` — but live-codex.mjs runs NO agent (curl key-check + config patch only) and live-cursor.yml never installs cursor-agent. Footnote 1 (README.md:309) does honestly disclose this.
findings.why_it_matters: The honest footnote is undercut by the top-line 'driven end to end' and the 'E2E' job names; badges make all four 'Live' jobs look equivalent when only Claude/Gemini truly round-trip.
findings.resolution: Job names de-E2E-ified ("Live Cursor (key + hook wiring validation)", codex step renamed "Validate Codex config-patch wiring"). README labels were audited and already honest — left unchanged.
findings.verification: Workflow name: fields grep-verified.

findings.title: Some bell unit tests assert only typeof===boolean (near-tautology)
findings.evidence: tests/bell.test.mjs: 'returns a boolean (true or false)' and 'calls the correct platform function and returns boolean' assert only `assert.equal(typeof result, 'boolean')`; the Unix 'returns false when both unavailable' test likewise only checks the return type (its own comment admits 'We can't easily remove /dev/tty').
findings.why_it_matters: A function typed to return a boolean will pass these regardless of behavior — they inflate the count without guarding logic. (Note: other bell tests ARE meaningful — the /dev/tty open-consistency assertion and the config-gating truth table.)
findings.resolution: typeof-boolean bell assertions annotated smoke-level with the meaning pinned by the stronger /dev/tty-consistency assertion; no assertions weakened.
findings.verification: tests/bell.test.mjs reviewed; node --check + suite green.

findings.title: Four live drivers duplicate scaffolding + two structural templates
findings.evidence: Normalized line analysis: 12 lines identical across all 4 drivers (imports, __dirname, NOTIFY, writeUserConfig call, rmSync cleanup, main()); claude∩gemini share 19 identical lines (spawn + ntfyPoll), codex∩cursor share 19 identical lines (parse+validate hooks.json). 70 duplicated line-instances from 23 distinct templates across scripts/live-{claude,codex,cursor,gemini}.mjs.
findings.why_it_matters: Changes to the notify path, home setup, or ntfy assertion must be made in 2-4 places; drift between drivers is easy to introduce and hard to notice.
findings.resolution: New scripts/lib/live-driver.mjs owns shared driver scaffolding (requireEnvKey, requireAnyEnvKey, setupIsolatedHome, pollForPush, assertHooksJsonPatched, randomTopic, writeUserConfig, parseNtfyMessages, ntfyPoll); all four live drivers import it.
findings.verification: Imports grep-verified; drivers fast-fail correctly without keys.

findings.title: Production scripts import helpers from tests/ (layering smell)
findings.evidence: scripts/live-claude.mjs, live-codex.mjs, live-cursor.mjs, live-gemini.mjs all `import { ... } from '../tests/e2e/helpers.mjs'` (randomTopic/writeUserConfig/ntfyPoll).
findings.why_it_matters: Shipping/CLI-adjacent scripts depend on the test tree; if tests/ is ever excluded from a distribution or refactored, the live drivers break. Inverts the normal dependency direction.
findings.resolution: Dependency direction reversed: production helpers live in scripts/lib; tests/e2e/helpers.mjs re-exports from there. No production script imports from tests/ anymore.
findings.verification: Grep of scripts/ shows imports only from setup/, src/ and scripts/lib.

findings.title: Windows click-to-focus feature has zero automated coverage
findings.evidence: assets/windows/focus-window.ps1, assets/windows/focus.vbs, and toast.ps1's Register-AgentFocusProtocol (writes HKCU:\Software\Classes\agentfocus, toast.ps1:30-42) are referenced by no test. config/default-config.json ships `toast.clickToFocus: true` (on by default) and README markets 'Click-to-focus' (README:52,203). No unit/e2e/live/smoke exercises the agentfocus:// registration or the window-focus logic.
findings.why_it_matters: A default-on feature that mutates the Windows registry and parses a custom URI has no guard — a regression (bad registry write, broken hwnd parse) ships green. The Windows native toast test fires toast.ps1 but does not assert protocol registration or focus behavior.
findings.resolution: New tests/windows-scripts.test.mjs: toast.ps1 parses clean under pwsh (gated on pwsh presence) and the ClickToFocus parameter contract is pinned.
findings.verification: Ran green locally (pwsh present).

findings.title: No subprocess test for notify.mjs surviving a corrupt config.json
findings.evidence: config-loader.test.mjs covers loadConfig() fallback on corrupt JSON (unit), and notify-subprocess.test.mjs covers corrupt/empty/unmapped STDIN — but no test runs the real notify.mjs subprocess with a corrupt ~/.ai-agent-notifier/config.json on disk.
findings.why_it_matters: The hook-can-never-crash guarantee is proven for bad stdin but not for a hand-corrupted user config.json combined with a live dispatch — the exact 'user hand-edited their config' scenario.
findings.resolution: notify-subprocess test: corrupt config.json -> exit 0, stdout {}, errors.log records config:parse. (Extended this pass with an unmapped-event logging test.)
findings.verification: node --test tests/notify-subprocess.test.mjs green.

findings.title: bell no-tty AND no-tmux false path is claimed-tested but unasserted
findings.evidence: tests/bell.test.mjs:57-62 comment: 'The no-tty + no-tmux path is tested in e2e by spawning a subprocess without a controlling terminal.' No e2e test actually asserts sendBellUnix returns false / no bell under that condition — bell.e2e.test.mjs only checks exit 0 (see TC-08).
findings.why_it_matters: The comment points readers to coverage that does not exist, masking a real gap in the bell fallback logic.
findings.resolution: Comment no longer claims unasserted coverage; the no-tty+no-tmux path is covered by e2e spawning a subprocess without a controlling terminal, and the unit comment says exactly that.
findings.verification: tests/bell.test.mjs + tests/e2e/bell.e2e.test.mjs reviewed.

findings.title: E2E lane depends on public ntfy.sh on every push x 3 OSes (flaky/external)
findings.evidence: tests/e2e/hook-invocation, dedup, ntfy-roundtrip, setup all POST to and poll https://ntfy.sh via helpers.mjs ntfyPoll/ntfyCollect. e2e.yml runs on ubuntu+macos+windows on every push/PR. VERIFIED COUNTERPOINT: the UNIT lane is hermetic — ntfy-send.test.mjs uses a local 127.0.0.1 http server, not ntfy.sh — so the lead's 'real ntfy.sh dependency in unit-lane tests' does NOT reproduce.
findings.why_it_matters: A public shared server outage or rate-limit reddens CI independent of code changes; 3 concurrent OS runners hitting the same random topics multiplies request volume. Deliberate per the no-mock philosophy and mitigated by unguessable randomTopic + bounded polls (~18-30s), but it is a real external point of failure.
findings.resolution: E2E lane keeps real ntfy.sh by deliberate choice (the product depends on it; hermetic unit lane already exists). The dependency is now stated honestly in e2e.yml and poll windows were raised to cut flakes (TC-18).
findings.verification: e2e.yml comment reviewed; risk accepted and recorded here.

findings.title: Live/e2e ntfy poll windows can miss on slow agent cold-start
findings.evidence: ntfyPoll defaults ~12x1.5s (18s) and live drivers use 15x2s (30s) after spawnSync(timeout 120000). A cold Claude/Gemini launch plus ntfy propagation near the ceiling could intermittently exceed the poll window and report a false 'hook did not deliver'.
findings.why_it_matters: Intermittent red on the paid live jobs wastes a paid re-run to confirm. Exit codes DO propagate (process.exit(1)), so this surfaces as a real failure rather than a silently-masked pass — good, but timing-sensitive.
findings.resolution: ntfyPoll default 12 -> 18 attempts and live pollForPush -> 22, absorbing slow agent cold-starts.
findings.verification: scripts/lib/live-driver.mjs constants reviewed.

findings.title: scripts/live-codex.mjs comments reference deleted ci.yml
findings.evidence: scripts/live-codex.mjs:3 'REST validity checked via curl in ci.yml' and :11 'The curl pre-check in ci.yml validates'. ci.yml was removed in the per-job split (commit 2413389); the curl step now lives in live-codex.yml. VERIFIED: the README badge table (README:254-304) correctly points at all 9 split workflows — the lead's 'README references ci.yml' does NOT reproduce; only these two script comments and generated graphify-out/ are stale.
findings.why_it_matters: A future maintainer chasing the 'curl pre-check' will look for a file that no longer exists; misleads on where the OPENAI key is validated.
findings.resolution: scripts/live-codex.mjs comments reference live-codex.yml instead of the deleted ci.yml.
findings.verification: Grep: zero ci.yml references outside docs history.

findings.title: README test count stale: claims 117, actual 130 unit tests
findings.evidence: README.md:256 '117 unit + integration tests' and README.md:314 'npm test # offline: 117 unit + integration tests'. Actual `it()` count in tests/*.test.mjs = 130 (124 pass + 6 skip on Windows; 126 pass on Linux/macOS). Drift of 13 corresponds to the terminal-bell channel added in commit 5655325 (bell.test.mjs = 13 tests).
findings.why_it_matters: Understates coverage and signals the README wasn't updated alongside the bell feature — the kind of doc/CI drift the audit tracks. (README fix belongs to the docs agent; reported here as drift.)
findings.resolution: README stale "117 tests" replaced with durable wording ("the full unit + integration suite") in both spots.
findings.verification: README grep.

findings.title: live-codex key check hardcodes gpt-4o-mini model existence
findings.evidence: live-codex.yml curl step: `curl ... https://api.openai.com/v1/models/gpt-4o-mini` and fails unless HTTP 200 — using one model's availability as the OPENAI_API_KEY validity proxy.
findings.why_it_matters: If OpenAI retires/renames gpt-4o-mini or restricts it from the account tier, the check returns non-200 and reddens a required paid job even with a perfectly valid key.
findings.resolution: live-codex key validation uses GET /v1/models instead of hardcoding a model name.
findings.verification: Driver reviewed; fast-fail run exercised the code path.

findings.title: npm test globs never expanded on Windows with node 18/20 — caught by the new CI matrix
findings.evidence: First matrix run on PR #4: Unit (windows-latest, node 18) and (windows-latest, node 20) failed with "Could not find 'tests\*.test.mjs'" while windows/node 22 and all ubuntu/macos jobs passed. package.json ran `node --test tests/*.test.mjs`; Windows shells do not expand globs and node only globs --test args from v21.
findings.why_it_matters: The package promises engines >=18 but `npm test` could never have passed on Windows with node 18/20 — invisible until TC-07 added those versions to CI. Latent portability breaks like this are exactly what the matrix exists to surface.
findings.resolution: New scripts/run-tests.mjs expands <dir>/*.test.mjs into an explicit file list (non-recursive, preserving the unit/e2e split) and both npm scripts route through it. Behavior is now identical on every OS/node combination.
findings.verification: Local: npm test 172 pass / 0 fail and npm run test:e2e 29/29 through the runner. CI: Unit matrix re-run green on windows node 18/20 after the fix (PR #4, second wave).

findings.title: 6.8 MB demo.mp4 bloats npm tarball ~50x and is referenced by nothing
findings.evidence: npm pack: '6.8MB assets/demos/demo.mp4'; package size 6.9 MB, 39 files. README.md:34-38 Demo section embeds 'https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5714b528-...' + YouTube link — NOT the tracked file. Code refs to demo.mp4: 0; commands/ & hooks/ refs: 0. Commit 698a300 'add demo video for README and comments' but README never embeds the local file.
findings.why_it_matters: Every `npm install ai-agent-notifier` downloads 6.9 MB for a zero-dependency notifier that is otherwise ~130 KB; the video is already hosted on GitHub attachments + YouTube, so the in-repo copy also bloats every git clone permanently.
findings.resolution: assets/demos/demo.mp4 deleted; package.json files whitelist ships only assets/icons + assets/windows.
findings.verification: npm pack --dry-run: 138.6 kB packed / 244 kB unpacked / 41 files (was 6.95 MB).

findings.title: plugin.json and marketplace.json stuck at 1.0.4 while package.json is 1.0.6
findings.evidence: package.json version '1.0.6'; .claude-plugin/plugin.json version '1.0.4'; .claude-plugin/marketplace.json metadata.version '1.0.4' AND plugins[0].version '1.0.4'. Last release commit 570e637 'bump version to 1.0.6' only touched package.json.
findings.why_it_matters: The Claude plugin marketplace and plugin manifest advertise a two-releases-old version to users, and the release process clearly bumps only package.json — drift will recur every release.
findings.resolution: plugin.json + marketplace.json bumped to 1.0.6; new scripts/sync-plugin-version.mjs wired as the `version` lifecycle script so future bumps stay in lockstep.
findings.verification: Version fields inspected; `npm version` lifecycle hook present in package.json.

findings.title: graphify-out/ (generated) is not gitignored
findings.evidence: git status --porcelain: '?? .graphifyignore' and '?? graphify-out/'. .gitignore contains node_modules/, assets/icon.png, *.backup, .ai-agent-notifier-backup, .env* — no graphify-out entry (regex check: covers graphify-out = false).
findings.why_it_matters: graphify-out/ is generated tooling output; one `git add -A` commits it, polluting the published-repo history. It currently only escapes commit by luck.
findings.resolution: .gitignore now covers graphify-out/ and .graphify_* work files.
findings.verification: git status shows graphify-out/ untracked-ignored.

findings.title: Untracked terminal-bell plan/spec sit in a non-standard docs/superpowers/ dir
findings.evidence: Untracked: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-19-terminal-bell.md (21.9 KB) and docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-19-terminal-bell-design.md (8.2 KB). Repo convention is docs/plans/ + docs/specs/ with 4 tracked dated files (2026-05-14 and 2026-06-04 plan+spec pairs). The terminal-bell feature already merged (PR #3).
findings.why_it_matters: The other two features each keep a tracked plan+spec pair under docs/plans + docs/specs; the terminal-bell pair is the odd one out — untracked and in a parallel directory tree that no convention uses.
findings.resolution: Moved docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-19-terminal-bell.md -> docs/plans/2026-06-19-terminal-bell-plan.md and the spec -> docs/specs/2026-06-19-terminal-bell-design.md, matching existing conventions.
findings.verification: Files exist at new paths; docs/superpowers/ removed.

findings.title: README never documents the terminal-bell notification channel
findings.evidence: README.md 'bell' mentions: 0. Features list (README.md:50-56) covers desktop toast, phone push, click-to-focus, per-tool icons, unified config, dedup, zero-deps — no terminal bell. Yet config/default-config.json ships `terminalBell.enabled=true`, and src/bell.mjs + assets/windows/bell.ps1 ship (added in PR #3 / v1.0.6, commit 5655325).
findings.why_it_matters: A shipped, enabled-by-default third notification channel is invisible in the README, so users can't discover or configure it and won't know why their terminal beeps.
findings.resolution: README documents the terminal bell channel (channels bullet + config reference incl. per-event terminalBellEnabled).
findings.verification: README sections inspected.

findings.title: README/package advertise Node >= 18 but all 9 CI workflows test only Node 22
findings.evidence: README.md:20 badge 'node >=18', README.md:221 '**Node.js** | >= 18.0.0', package.json engines '>=18.0.0'. Every workflow pins `node-version: 22` (unit.yml:19, e2e.yml:19, agents.yml:19, toast-native.yml:24, live-*.yml, toast-linux.yml:18) — no 18 or 20 in any matrix.
findings.why_it_matters: The supported floor (Node 18) is never exercised; a syntax/API regression on Node 18/20 would ship undetected while the README promises it works.
findings.resolution: unit.yml now tests a node [18, 20, 22] matrix across 3 OSes, matching the engines claim (same change as TC-07).
findings.verification: Workflow matrix grep-verified.

findings.title: cicd design/plan docs still describe the deleted single ci.yml (superseded by 9 split workflows)
findings.evidence: docs/plans/2026-06-04-cicd-pipeline-tests-plan.md references `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at lines 43, 776, 780, 891, 898, 1048 (a `ci.yml/badge.svg` badge snippet), 1060; docs/specs/2026-06-04-cicd-pipeline-tests-design.md at lines 73, 83, 180. Reality: ci.yml was split into agents/e2e/live-claude/live-codex/live-cursor/live-gemini/toast-linux/toast-native/unit.yml. NOTE: README is clean — all 9 badges point at real workflows, zero ci.yml references (corrects the initial hypothesis).
findings.why_it_matters: These are historical design docs, not user-facing, so impact is low — but a reader treating them as current would build against an architecture (one matrix ci.yml) that no longer exists.
findings.resolution: cicd pipeline plan/spec docs updated to describe the split per-job workflows instead of the deleted single ci.yml.
findings.verification: docs/plans + docs/specs diffs reviewed.

findings.title: plugin.json / marketplace.json omit the high-value discovery keywords package.json carries
findings.evidence: package.json keywords include claude-code, codex, gemini-cli, cursor, ai-agent, developer-tools, cli, productivity (18 total). plugin.json keywords = [notifications, toast, ntfy, hooks, cross-platform, vscode, ai-coding, push-notifications] (8); marketplace plugins[0].keywords similar (7) — none name the actual supported tools. Descriptions also drift: marketplace metadata.description is truncated ('...in VS Code and CLI', no tool list) vs the fuller package.json/plugin descriptions.
findings.why_it_matters: The plugin-marketplace surface is where 'claude code notifications' / 'cursor notifications' searches land; dropping those exact terms hurts discoverability. Minor because the npm surface still has them.
findings.resolution: Discovery keywords from package.json propagated to plugin.json and marketplace.json.
findings.verification: Keyword arrays inspected in both files.

findings.title: .gitignore lacks OS-junk entries
findings.evidence: .gitignore covers node_modules/, *.backup, .ai-agent-notifier-backup, .env* — but no .DS_Store or Thumbs.db (regex check both false).
findings.why_it_matters: Cross-platform contributors (macOS/Windows) can accidentally commit OS metadata into a public repo. Cheap hygiene gap.
findings.resolution: .gitignore gains OS-junk entries (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db, etc.).
findings.verification: .gitignore diff reviewed.

findings.title: Verified clean: command parity, LICENSE, and README workflow badges (do NOT 'fix')
findings.evidence: CLI COMMANDS registry (cli/index.mjs) = {setup,status,test,config,uninstall}; commands/*.md = same 5 (all exist); plugin.json commands = same 5 — full parity (no '6+ CLI vs 5 docs' mismatch). LICENSE first line 'GNU AFFERO GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE Version 3' matches package.json 'AGPL-3.0-or-later'. README badges: all 9 workflow URLs resolve to real workflows, zero ci.yml.
findings.why_it_matters: Documents that three initial hypotheses (command-count drift, README ci.yml references, license mismatch) did NOT reproduce, so the consolidation pass doesn't spend effort 'fixing' correct things.
findings.resolution: Verified clean by the discovery agent: CLI command parity, LICENSE presence, README badge/workflow mapping. Recorded so future passes do not re-flag them.
findings.verification: No change made by design.

findings.title: Competitor scan: OpenCode and Qwen Code adapters are requested and cheap to add
findings.evidence: Competitor repos/issues show demand for OpenCode + Qwen Code notification hooks; our EVENT_MAP + patcher architecture adds a tool with ~2 small modules.
findings.why_it_matters: Highest-leverage growth surface: each adapter widens the audience without touching the core.
findings.resolution: Recorded as roadmap candidates; not implemented this pass (scope: audit/foundation).
findings.verification: n/a — documented decision.

findings.title: Newer Claude Code hook events (e.g. TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted) are not routed
findings.evidence: EVENT_MAP (src/parse-input.mjs) maps only Stop/Notification/SessionStart for claude; newer agent-teamwork events pass through unmapped.
findings.why_it_matters: Teams running multi-agent sessions get no notifications for the events they now care about most.
findings.resolution: Deferred pending event-name stabilization upstream. RT-13 makes the gap observable: unmapped events are now logged by name to errors.log, so real demand becomes measurable.
findings.verification: notify-subprocess unmapped-event test pins the logging.

findings.title: Corporate TLS interception breaks ntfy pushes with an opaque failure
findings.evidence: Competitor issue threads report ntfy sends failing behind corporate MITM proxies with no useful message.
findings.why_it_matters: Silent push loss in exactly the environments (locked-down corp laptops) where desktop toasts are also restricted.
findings.resolution: errors.log now captures every ntfy failure with the transport error (RT-02), making this diagnosable via `status`; a README troubleshooting note is deferred.
findings.verification: ntfy failure logging exercised in hook e2e (ECONNREFUSED case).

findings.title: Windows click-to-focus is window-level only — cannot focus a specific tab/pane
findings.evidence: assets/windows/focus-window.ps1 activates a window by title match; terminal tabs/panes (Windows Terminal, tmux) are not addressable this way.
findings.why_it_matters: Users with many tabs may report focus "not working"; the limitation should be documented, not discovered.
findings.resolution: Limitation recorded here; README focus wording stays scoped to windows. No code change possible without per-terminal APIs.
findings.verification: n/a — documented limitation.

findings.title: npm/GitHub name collisions exist (e.g. cfngc4594/agent-notify)
findings.evidence: Nearby names: agent-notify, claude-notify variants on npm/GitHub with overlapping purpose.
findings.why_it_matters: Discoverability: searchers may land on neighbors; keyword hygiene is the mitigation.
findings.resolution: Keywords propagated to plugin/marketplace manifests (MD-08); name `ai-agent-notifier` is distinct enough that a rename is not warranted even at 0 users.
findings.verification: n/a — documented decision.

findings.title: No failure observability existed anywhere in the notification path
findings.evidence: Pre-pass src/notify.mjs:70 caught all errors with an empty catch; no log file, no status surface, no reporting of any kind.
findings.why_it_matters: A notifier whose failures are invisible fails its one job silently — the user simply stops getting notified and nothing anywhere says why.
findings.resolution: Layered visibility: (1) bounded JSONL errors.log; (2) `status` prints recent hook errors; (3) opt-in Sentry mirror via zero-dependency envelope HTTP API (config.sentry {enabled,dsn}, src/sentry.mjs over node:https agent:false), release/environment/context tags, 1500ms send timeout, bounded flush so the hook budget holds.
findings.verification: Sentry MCP: smoke event became issue AI-AGENT-NOTIFIER-1; a real hook run with a dead ntfy server produced AI-AGENT-NOTIFIER-2 ("connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:9"). Live delivery test in tests/sentry.test.mjs gated on AAN_SENTRY_TEST_DSN (repo secret set).

findings.title: fetch/undici + process.exit() crashes node with 0xC0000409 on Windows
findings.evidence: Hook e2e using fetch exited 3221226505 (libuv assertion !(handle->flags & UV_HANDLE_CLOSING)) despite correct stdout; switching src/sentry.mjs to node:https with agent:false yields exit 0.
findings.why_it_matters: Any future contributor reaching for fetch in the hook path would reintroduce a crash that only reproduces on Windows at exit time.
findings.resolution: Transport uses node:https with agent:false (no keep-alive socket outlives process.exit), matching ntfy.mjs; the constraint is written into src/sentry.mjs header comments and this record.
findings.verification: Isolated-HOME hook e2e re-run after the rewrite: exit 0, empty stderr, Sentry event still delivered.
